infobox.value: Синьо-жовті / *Synʹo-zhovti* (The Blue and Yellow) Збірна / *Zbirna* (The National Team)
[Ukrainian Association of Football](/wiki/Ukrainian_Association_of_Football "Ukrainian Association of Football") (UAF) Українська Асоціація Футболу
[Andriy Shevchenko](/wiki/Andriy_Shevchenko "Andriy Shevchenko") ([48](/wiki/List_of_international_goals_scored_by_Andriy_Shevchenko "List of international goals scored by Andriy Shevchenko"))

from the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine") - [Various](#Stadiums) in other countries (Poland, Czech Republic, Slovakia, Germany, Spain)

([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Збірна України з футболу, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ukrainian "Romanization of Ukrainian"): *Zbirna Ukrainy z futbolu*) represents [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") in men's international [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), and is governed by the [Ukrainian Association of Football](/wiki/Ukrainian_Association_of_Football "Ukrainian Association of Football"), the governing body for [football in Ukraine](/wiki/Football_in_Ukraine "Football in Ukraine"). Ukraine's home ground is the [Olimpiyskiy Stadium](/wiki/Olimpiyskiy_National_Sports_Complex "Olimpiyskiy National Sports Complex") in [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"). The team has been a full member of [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") and [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") since 1992.

The **Ukraine national football team** ([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Збірна України з футболу, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ukrainian "Romanization of Ukrainian"): *Zbirna Ukrainy z futbolu*) represents [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") in men's international [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), and is governed by the [Ukrainian Association of Football](/wiki/Ukrainian_Association_of_Football "Ukrainian Association of Football"), the governing body for [football in Ukraine](/wiki/Football_in_Ukraine "Football in Ukraine"). Ukraine's home ground is the [Olimpiyskiy Stadium](/wiki/Olimpiyskiy_National_Sports_Complex "Olimpiyskiy National Sports Complex") in [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"). The team has been a full member of [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") and [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") since 1992.

After [the restoration of the Ukrainian independence](/wiki/Declaration_of_Independence_of_Ukraine "Declaration of Independence of Ukraine"), the team played their [first match](/wiki/1992_Ukraine_v_Hungary_football_match "1992 Ukraine v Hungary football match") against [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary_national_football_team "Hungary national football team") on 29 April 1992. In their debut in a major world championship, the team reached the quarter-finals in the [2006 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup").[[3]](#cite_note-UEFA-4) In addition to Russia and Uzbekistan, Ukraine is the only post-Soviet state to qualify for the FIFA World Cup finals.

As the host nation, Ukraine automatically qualified for [UEFA Euro 2012](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012 "UEFA Euro 2012").[[3]](#cite_note-UEFA-4) Four years later, Ukraine finished third in their qualifying group for [Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016") and advanced for the first time, via the play-off route and qualifiers, to reach a [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship") tournament. This marked the first time in Ukraine's six play-off appearances that it managed to win a tie, having lost previous play-off ties for the [1998 World Cup](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup"), [Euro 2000](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000 "UEFA Euro 2000"), the [2002 World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup"), the [2010 World Cup](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup "2010 FIFA World Cup") and the [2014 World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup"), and would lose again in the [2022 World Cup](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup "2022 FIFA World Cup") play-offs.

Ukraine's best performances in the UEFA European Championship and in the [World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") were in [2020](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020 "UEFA Euro 2020") and [2006](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup") respectively, reaching the quarter-finals for the first time in both cases.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukraine_national_football_team&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Ukrainian SSR (1924–1990)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukraine_national_football_team&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ukrainian SSR (1924–1990)")]

The national team was formed in the early 1990s and was recognized internationally soon thereafter. Ukraine, under the [Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic"), previously had a national team in 1924–1935[[4]](#cite_note-5)[[5]](#cite_note-6) just like the [Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic"). The national team included the players Andriy Ponomarenko, [Ivan Privalov](/wiki/Ivan_Privalov_(footballer) "Ivan Privalov (footballer)"), [Volodymyr Fomin](/wiki/Volodymyr_Fomin "Volodymyr Fomin"), H. Syrota, [Mykola Fomin](/wiki/Mykola_Fomin "Mykola Fomin"), Anatoliy Lisnyi, Oleksandr Shatokha (goalkeeper), Dmytro Kyryllov, Dmytro Starusev, Serhiy Kopeiko, Petro Parovyshnykov (first team); Valentyn Prokofyev, Fedir Tyutchev, H. Yakubovskyi, Ivan Vladymyrskyi, Serafim Moskvin (goalkeeper), Kazymyr Piontkovskyi, Mykhailo Pashuta, Vasiliy Yepishin, Adam Bem, K. Us, Volodymyr Prasolov (second team).[[6]](#cite_note-7)[[7]](#cite_note-8)

The earliest record of games played by Ukraine can be traced back to August 1928. A championship among the national teams of the Soviet republics as well as the [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow") city team took place in Moscow; at the All-Soviet tournament, Ukraine reached the final where it lost to Moscow 1–0, after defeating [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus_national_football_team "Belarus national football team") and [Transcaucasus](/wiki/Transcaucasian_Socialist_Federative_Soviet_Republic "Transcaucasian Socialist Federative Soviet Republic").

In 1929, Ukraine beat [Lower Austria](/wiki/Lower_Austria "Lower Austria") in an exhibition match in [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv") 4–1, and played in another Soviet tournament. Ukraine lost to Transcaucasus 3–0.

### Official formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukraine_national_football_team&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Official formation")]

Before 1991, Ukrainian players were represented by the [Soviet Union national team](/wiki/Soviet_Union_national_football_team "Soviet Union national football team"). After the collapse of the [USSR](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") in 1991, [Russia](/wiki/Russia_national_football_team "Russia national football team") took its place in the qualifying tournament for the 1994 World Cup. The Ukraine national team did not enter the tournament[[8]](#cite_note-9) as it was not yet admitted to FIFA. Meanwhile, some of the best Ukrainian players of the 1990s (including [Andrei Kanchelskis](/wiki/Andrei_Kanchelskis "Andrei Kanchelskis"), [Viktor Onopko](/wiki/Viktor_Onopko "Viktor Onopko"), [Sergei Yuran](/wiki/Sergei_Yuran "Sergei Yuran"), [Yuriy Nikiforov](/wiki/Yuriy_Nikiforov "Yuriy Nikiforov"), [Ilya Tsymbalar](/wiki/Ilya_Tsymbalar "Ilya Tsymbalar") and [Oleg Salenko](/wiki/Oleg_Salenko "Oleg Salenko")) chose to play for Russia. At the time, [Vyacheslav Koloskov](/wiki/Vyacheslav_Koloskov "Vyacheslav Koloskov"), a top official from the former Soviet Union and later [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), served as a vice-president of [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") from 1980–1996 representing the Soviet Union and later the [Commonwealth of Independent States](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Independent_States "Commonwealth of Independent States") (CIS).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Valeri_Lobanovsky.jpg/250px-Valeri_Lobanovsky.jpg)](/wiki/File:Valeri_Lobanovsky.jpg)

[Valeriy Lobanovskyi](/wiki/Valeriy_Lobanovskyi "Valeriy Lobanovskyi") was Head Coach of the National Team in 1979 and between 2001 and 2002.

The Soviet Union's five-year [UEFA coefficient](/wiki/UEFA_coefficient "UEFA coefficient") (earned in part by Ukrainian players), was transferred to the Russian national team. As a result, a crisis was created for both the national team and the [domestic league](/wiki/Ukrainian_Premier_League "Ukrainian Premier League").

Controversy developed because the Ukrainian football league lacked adequate funding for their teams[[9]](#cite_note-kmcrossing-10) due to the economic crisis affecting all CIS countries.[[9]](#cite_note-kmcrossing-10) There also was a reverse influx of players;[[9]](#cite_note-kmcrossing-10) [Viktor Leonenko](/wiki/Viktor_Leonenko "Viktor Leonenko") agreed on transfer from [Dynamo Moscow](/wiki/Dynamo_Moscow "Dynamo Moscow") to [Dynamo Kyiv](/wiki/FC_Dynamo_Kyiv "FC Dynamo Kyiv"). The Russian club did not want to release him, but Leonenko did not want to continue to play in Moscow.[[9]](#cite_note-kmcrossing-10)

In the following years, the Ukrainian team improved, including talents like [Andriy Shevchenko](/wiki/Andriy_Shevchenko "Andriy Shevchenko"), [Serhiy Rebrov](/wiki/Serhiy_Rebrov "Serhiy Rebrov"), and [Oleksandr Shovkovskyi](/wiki/Oleksandr_Shovkovskyi "Oleksandr Shovkovskyi").

### First official games (Prokopenko)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukraine_national_football_team&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: First official games (Prokopenko)")]

In 1992, Ukraine was accepted as a full member to [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") and [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") at which time Ukraine selected its first manager by members of a coaching council which consisted of [Anatoliy Puzach](/wiki/Anatoliy_Puzach "Anatoliy Puzach") (manager of [Dynamo Kyiv](/wiki/FC_Dynamo_Kyiv "FC Dynamo Kyiv")), [Yevhen Kucherevskyi](/wiki/Yevhen_Kucherevskyi "Yevhen Kucherevskyi") ([FC Dnipro](/wiki/FC_Dnipro "FC Dnipro")), [Yevhen Lemeshko](/wiki/Yevhen_Lemeshko "Yevhen Lemeshko") ([Torpedo Zaporizhzhia](/wiki/FC_Torpedo_Zaporizhzhia "FC Torpedo Zaporizhzhia")), [Yukhym Shkolnykov](/wiki/Yukhym_Shkolnykov "Yukhym Shkolnykov") ([Bukovyna Chernivtsi](/wiki/FSC_Bukovyna_Chernivtsi "FSC Bukovyna Chernivtsi")) and [Viktor Prokopenko](/wiki/Viktor_Prokopenko "Viktor Prokopenko") ([Chornomorets Odesa](/wiki/FC_Chornomorets_Odesa "FC Chornomorets Odesa")). Later, they were joined by [Valeriy Yaremchenko](/wiki/Valeriy_Yaremchenko "Valeriy Yaremchenko") ([Shakhtar Donetsk](/wiki/FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk "FC Shakhtar Donetsk")). The circle was narrowed to three specialists and Prokopenko eventually became the manager.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/%D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D1%8F_%D0%A4%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE._%D0%94%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B5%D1%86%D0%BA._%D0%A3%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%8C_%D0%B8_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%BB%D1%8E%D0%B1%D0%B8%D1%82%D1%8C._%D0%A4%D0%9A_%D0%A8%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80_051.jpg/250px-%D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D1%8F_%D0%A4%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE._%D0%94%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B5%D1%86%D0%BA._%D0%A3%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%8C_%D0%B8_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%BB%D1%8E%D0%B1%D0%B8%D1%82%D1%8C._%D0%A4%D0%9A_%D0%A8%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80_051.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D1%8F_%D0%A4%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE._%D0%94%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B5%D1%86%D0%BA._%D0%A3%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%8C_%D0%B8_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%BB%D1%8E%D0%B1%D0%B8%D1%82%D1%8C._%D0%A4%D0%9A_%D0%A8%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80_051.jpg)

[Viktor Prokopenko](/wiki/Viktor_Prokopenko "Viktor Prokopenko"), the first official manager of the national team

Ukraine played their first match on 29 April 1992 against Hungary in [Uzhhorod](/wiki/Uzhhorod "Uzhhorod") at the [Avanhard Stadium](/wiki/Avanhard_Stadium_(Uzhhorod) "Avanhard Stadium (Uzhhorod)"), losing 3–1 with the sole Ukrainian goal scored by [Ivan Hetsko](/wiki/Ivan_Hetsko "Ivan Hetsko"). With the creation of a "phantom" (transitional) [CIS team](/wiki/CIS_national_football_team "CIS national football team") in place of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_national_football_team "Soviet Union national football team") playing against [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") in Moscow in preparation for the [UEFA Euro 1992](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1992 "UEFA Euro 1992").[[11]](#cite_note-12) That year the Ukrainian team lost some notable players to the Soviet Union team. Following several losses to Hungary and a draw to the United States, Prokopenko resigned and the last season game was led by his assistants [Mykola Pavlov](/wiki/Mykola_Pavlov "Mykola Pavlov") and [Leonid Tkachenko](/wiki/Leonid_Tkachenko_(footballer) "Leonid Tkachenko (footballer)").

### Euro 1996 qualification (Bazylevych)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukraine_national_football_team&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Euro 1996 qualification (Bazylevych)")]

Main article: [UEFA Euro 1996 qualifying Group 4](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996_qualifying_Group_4 "UEFA Euro 1996 qualifying Group 4")

Ukraine appointed head coach, [Oleh Bazylevych](/wiki/Oleh_Bazylevych "Oleh Bazylevych"), who made his debut with the national team in the spring of 1993 in [Odesa](/wiki/Odesa "Odesa") during a friendly game against [Israel](/wiki/Israel_national_football_team "Israel national football team"), resulting in a 1–1 draw. Less than one month later, Ukraine finally won in [Vilnius](/wiki/Vilnius "Vilnius") against Lithuania. During that summer they lost 3–1 to Croatia; Ukraine was later seeded in [Group 4](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996_qualifying#Group_4 "UEFA Euro 1996 qualifying") of the [UEFA Euro 1996](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996 "UEFA Euro 1996") qualification.

Ukraine was defeated by Israel in March 1994, and drew Bulgaria and the United Arab Emirates. On 7 September 1994, the national team started its first official qualification campaign with a home loss 2–0 to Lithuania.[[12]](#cite_note-13) Following the defeat and a weak performances in preceding friendlies, Bazylevych was fired and the tour to South Korea was led by the Bazylevych assistants [Pavlov](/wiki/Mykola_Pavlov "Mykola Pavlov") and [Muntyan](/wiki/Volodymyr_Muntyan "Volodymyr Muntyan"). Soon thereafter the Federation signed a contract with [Valeriy Lobanovsky](/wiki/Valeriy_Lobanovsky "Valeriy Lobanovsky"). On 24 September, the [Football Federation of Ukraine](/wiki/Ukrainian_Association_of_Football "Ukrainian Association of Football") appointed [Yozhef Sabo](/wiki/Yozhef_Sabo "Yozhef Sabo") as an acting manager until the end of the year after Lobanovsky signed a contract with [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait_national_football_team "Kuwait national football team").

Under Sabo's management, the team's next home game against [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia_national_football_team "Slovenia national football team") ended goalless[[13]](#cite_note-14) and they eventually beat Estonia 3–0, gaining their first win in an official competitive game.[[14]](#cite_note-15) At the beginning of the year the Football Federation confirmed [Anatoliy Konkov](/wiki/Anatoliy_Konkov "Anatoliy Konkov") as the new head coach on 5 January 1995.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Oleg_Blokhin-ua.jpeg/250px-Oleg_Blokhin-ua.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Oleg_Blokhin-ua.jpeg)

[Oleg Blokhin](/wiki/Oleg_Blokhin "Oleg Blokhin"), two spells in charge of the Ukraine national team

Under coach Konkov, the team started with away losses of 4–0 to Croatia and 3–0 to Italy.[[15]](#cite_note-16) Following the losses, there was a three-game winning streak including a home victory against Croatia. A subsequent loss to Slovenia had the team finishing in fourth place in its first qualification campaign behind Lithuania.

### 1998–2004: near misses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukraine_national_football_team&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1998–2004: near misses")]

Following Konkov's departure in 1996, the Federation appointed Sabo as head coach. Separately, there was a preliminary agreement with Lobanovsky to coach following his contract end with Kuwait.

Ukraine participated in [1998 World Cup qualification](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "1998 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)"), where the team was drawn into [Group 9](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_9 "1998 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 9"). Ukraine took second place, behind [Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team") and ahead of [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team"), but was defeated in a play-off stage 3–1 on aggregate by [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia_national_football_team "Croatia national football team"). The qualification campaign became notable as the beginning of the international career of [Shevchenko](/wiki/Andriy_Shevchenko "Andriy Shevchenko") as well as providing more play time for players such as [Oleksandr Shovkovsky](/wiki/Oleksandr_Shovkovsky "Oleksandr Shovkovsky") and [Serhiy Rebrov](/wiki/Serhiy_Rebrov "Serhiy Rebrov").

In the [UEFA Euro 2000 qualifying](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2000 qualifying"), Ukraine, assigned in [Group 4](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000_qualifying_Group_4 "UEFA Euro 2000 qualifying Group 4"), finished above [Russia](/wiki/Russia_national_football_team "Russia national football team"), thanks to an important draw in [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow") and a home victory. However, they still only qualified for the playoff behind the [French side](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team") despite being undefeated. Ukraine then fell to [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia_national_football_team "Slovenia national football team") 3–2 on aggregate. Following the qualification campaign, the Federation finally signed a contract with [Valery Lobanovsky](/wiki/Valery_Lobanovsky "Valery Lobanovsky"), ending Sabo's tenure as a head coach.

The [2002 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "2002 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)") saw Ukraine in [Group 5](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_5 "2002 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 5"). With Lobanovsky as a head coach, there were expectations of the first qualification to the finals. Yet, Ukraine suffered a home loss to [Poland](/wiki/Poland_national_football_team "Poland national football team") in their opening match, and many draws resulted in Ukraine qualifying for the playoff, losing to Germany, 5–2 on aggregate. Under public pressure, particularly the [Higher League](/wiki/Ukrainian_Premier_League "Ukrainian Premier League"), head coaches argued for the removal of Lobanovsky and the Federation decided not to renew its contract with Lobanovsky, instead moving him to [Dynamo Kyiv](/wiki/FC_Dynamo_Kyiv "FC Dynamo Kyiv").

In the [UEFA Euro 2004 qualifying](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2004 qualifying"), Ukraine with the new head coach [Leonid Buryak](/wiki/Leonid_Buryak "Leonid Buryak"), was assigned into [Group 6](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004_qualifying_Group_6 "UEFA Euro 2004 qualifying Group 6"), with [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team") and [Greece](/wiki/Greece_national_football_team "Greece national football team"). Ukraine failed to qualify.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### 2006 World Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukraine_national_football_team&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2006 World Cup")]

After the [Euro 2004 qualifying](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2004 qualifying") match, Ukraine appointed [Oleg Blokhin](/wiki/Oleg_Blokhin "Oleg Blokhin") as the national team's head coach. Placed in [Group 2](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_2 "2006 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 2"), Ukraine went on to qualify as a group winner for their first-ever [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") on 3 September 2005, after drawing 1–1 against [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_national_football_team "Georgia national football team") in [Tbilisi](/wiki/Tbilisi "Tbilisi") (and ahead of Turkey), playing Denmark and their last campaign against rivals Greece (among others). This was the first successful qualification campaign for Ukraine despite a poor home turf performance.

In the [2006 World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup"), Ukraine was in [Group H](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup_Group_H "2006 FIFA World Cup Group H") with [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team"), [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia_national_football_team "Tunisia national football team") and [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia_national_football_team "Saudi Arabia national football team"). After losing 4–0 in the first match against Spain, the Ukrainians won the next two matches to face [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_national_football_team "Switzerland national football team") in round 16. Drawing goalless, Ukraine took Switzerland to a penalty shoot-out where two saves from [Oleksandr Shovkovsky](/wiki/Oleksandr_Shovkovsky "Oleksandr Shovkovsky") secured a positive outcome for his side despite the first kick miss by [Andriy Shevchenko](/wiki/Andriy_Shevchenko "Andriy Shevchenko"). Switzerland which did not lose or yield a single goal was sent home early with Ukraine advancing to the quarterfinals. In the quarterfinals, Ukraine, facing [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team"), was defeated with two second half two goals from [Luca Toni](/wiki/Luca_Toni "Luca Toni"), securing a comfortable 3–0 win for the future 2006 World Cup champions.

### 2006–2012

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukraine_national_football_team&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2006–2012")]

After the World Cup, Ukraine was placed in [UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying Group B](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_qualifying_Group_B "UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying Group B"), along with [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team") and [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team"); Ukraine had also performed poorly against [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team"), [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_national_football_team "Georgia national football team") and [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania_national_football_team "Lithuania national football team"), ultimately finishing in fourth place. Due to the bleak performance of the national team [Oleg Blokhin](/wiki/Oleg_Blokhin "Oleg Blokhin") resigned and surprisingly signed with the recently established [FC Moscow](/wiki/FC_Moscow "FC Moscow").[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)

With another Soviet football star player [Oleksiy Mykhaylychenko](/wiki/Oleksiy_Mykhaylychenko "Oleksiy Mykhaylychenko") as the new head coach, [2010 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "2010 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)") saw Ukraine in [Group 6](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_6 "2010 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 6"), drawing [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia_national_football_team "Croatia national football team") and winning against [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team"), sending Ukraine to the playoff. Greece, which had been eliminated by Ukraine in the qualifiers four years earlier, would eventually get revenge.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Following the failure to qualify, the Federation decided not to renew the contract with Mykhaylychenko.[[18]](#cite_note-formerUK-19)

Main article: [UEFA Euro 2012 Group D](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012_Group_D "UEFA Euro 2012 Group D")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Ukraine_national_football_team_20120611.jpg/250px-Ukraine_national_football_team_20120611.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ukraine_national_football_team_20120611.jpg)

Ukraine in 2012

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Ukraine_national_team_in_2012.jpg/250px-Ukraine_national_team_in_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ukraine_national_team_in_2012.jpg)

Ukraine before a match against Bulgaria, 14 December 2012

As co-hosts, Ukraine qualified automatically for [Euro 2012](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012 "UEFA Euro 2012"),[[3]](#cite_note-UEFA-4) marking their debut in the [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship"). The Federation decided to appoint [Myron Markevych](/wiki/Myron_Markevych "Myron Markevych") to prepare and lead the national team in the Euro finals. However, following a few friendlies Markevych resigned due to the off-pitch politics and having held coaching office of both the national team and [Metalist Kharkiv](/wiki/FC_Metalist_Kharkiv "FC Metalist Kharkiv").[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21) For the next several games in 2010 and 2011 the national team was led by caretaker [Yuriy Kalitvintsev](/wiki/Yuriy_Kalitvintsev "Yuriy Kalitvintsev") who starred for Ukraine back in its first qualification campaign for the Euro 1996.

On 21 April 2011, Blokhin was again appointed head coach of the Ukraine national team signing a four-year contract.[[21]](#cite_note-Blokhin2011A-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23) With Blokhin at helm in their opening game against [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_football_team "Sweden men's national football team"), Ukraine won 2–1 in Kyiv. In Donetsk, Ukraine was eliminated after a 2–0 loss to France and a 1–0 defeat to England.

### 2014–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukraine_national_football_team&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2014–present")]

Drawn into [UEFA Group H](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_H "2014 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group H") for [2014 World Cup](/wiki/2014_World_Cup "2014 World Cup") qualification, Ukraine played against France, beating France at home 2–0 but suffered a 3–0 loss away. Blokhin stepped down due to health concerns in the autumn of 2012 soon after the first home game against [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") and was replaced by [Andriy Bal](/wiki/Andriy_Bal "Andriy Bal")[[23]](#cite_note-24) and later [Oleksandr Zavarov](/wiki/Oleksandr_Zavarov "Oleksandr Zavarov").

In 2012, the Federation appointed [Mykhailo Fomenko](/wiki/Mykhailo_Fomenko "Mykhailo Fomenko") as a head coach .[[24]](#cite_note-25) Even though the team under Fomenko did not manage to qualify for the World Cup, the Federation decided to retain his services until the end of 2015.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Ukraine_vs_Luxembourg_14062015_UEFA_EURO_2016_Qualifying_round_-_Group_C_%286%29.jpg/250px-Ukraine_vs_Luxembourg_14062015_UEFA_EURO_2016_Qualifying_round_-_Group_C_%286%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ukraine_vs_Luxembourg_14062015_UEFA_EURO_2016_Qualifying_round_-_Group_C_(6).jpg)

Ukraine in 2015

During ongoing [conflicts with Russian](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War"), Ukraine in [Euro 2016 qualifying](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying") was drawn against Spain, [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_national_football_team "Slovakia national football team"), Belarus, [Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia_national_football_team "North Macedonia national football team") and [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg_national_football_team "Luxembourg national football team"). Despite having won all matches except those against Spain and Slovakia, Ukraine finished third. They defeated [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia_national_football_team "Slovenia national football team") in the playoff, marking their first qualification for a major tournament through the playoffs.

Ukraine lost all three games at Euro 2016 without scoring a goal.

Following the Euro 2016, Fomenko was replaced with [Andriy Shevchenko](/wiki/Andriy_Shevchenko "Andriy Shevchenko") as head coach (who was his assistant during the Euro finals).[[26]](#cite_note-27) Seeded in the [UEFA Group I](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_I "2018 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group I"), Ukraine started with a home draw to [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland_national_football_team "Iceland national football team") in 2018 World Cup qualifying and an away draw to [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team") but lost to Croatia failing to qualify for the playoffs for the first time since [UEFA Euro 2004](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2004 qualifying").

In the [inaugural UEFA Nations League](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_UEFA_Nations_League_B "2018–19 UEFA Nations League B"), Ukraine was drawn with the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_national_football_team "Czech Republic national football team") and Slovakia in League B. They beat the Czech Republic 2–1 away and Slovakia 1–0 at home, before earning a promotion to League A with a 1–0 home win to the Czech Republic.

Main article: [UEFA Euro 2020 qualifying Group B](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020_qualifying_Group_B "UEFA Euro 2020 qualifying Group B")

Ukraine was placed in a group with Euro 2016 champions [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team") as well as [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia_national_football_team "Serbia national football team").

As the [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") crisis in [Ukraine](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Ukraine "COVID-19 pandemic in Ukraine") worsened, eight players from the starting squad tested positive (including one positive [SARS-CoV-2](/wiki/Severe_acute_respiratory_syndrome_coronavirus_2 "Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2") test upon arrival to [Lucerne](/wiki/Lucerne "Lucerne")), as a result the entire delegation was put into quarantine by the Department of Health of the [Canton of Lucerne](/wiki/Canton_of_Lucerne "Canton of Lucerne").[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29) Their game against Switzerland away was subsequently cancelled. UEFA decided that the match result would be 3–0 in favour of Switzerland and the [CAS](/wiki/Court_of_Arbitration_for_Sport "Court of Arbitration for Sport") subsequently upheld the result, meaning that Ukraine had been officially relegated after just one year in League A.[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31)

Main article: [UEFA Euro 2020](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020 "UEFA Euro 2020")

Ukraine qualified for the knockout stages in the European Championship as one of the best third-placed teams for the first time in the 2020 tournament, postponed to 2021 due to [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Europe "COVID-19 pandemic in Europe"). They beat Sweden 2–1 in the round of 16,and were then defeated by England in the quarter-final, recording their best finish at a major tournament since 2006.

In August 2021, Shevchenko[[31]](#cite_note-32) was replaced with [Oleksandr Petrakov](/wiki/Oleksandr_Petrakov "Oleksandr Petrakov"). In the [2022 World Cup qualifying](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_D "2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group D") rounds, Ukraine drew 1–1 in both games against France.[[32]](#cite_note-33) Ukraine would qualify for the playoff after breaking the record set by [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team") for the most consecutive draws in World Cup qualification, with five straight draws.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Ukraine eventually picked up a much-needed victory over Finland, ending their run of draws and giving them a two-point lead over Bosnia and a three-point lead over Finland. However, both Bosnia and Finland had a game in hand over Ukraine, who qualified for the playoffs after a 2–0 win over Bosnia and a Finnish loss to France. Ukraine faced [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team") in the Group A playoff semifinals, postponed in March 2022 to June after [Russia invaded the country in February](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"),[[33]](#cite_note-Decisions_on_WC_qualifiers-34) winning 3–1 at [Hampden Park](/wiki/Hampden_Park "Hampden Park"), but ultimately losing 1–0 to [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team") in an emotional playoff final at the [Cardiff City Stadium](/wiki/Cardiff_City_Stadium "Cardiff City Stadium").[[34]](#cite_note-35)

Ukraine, who were [relegated to League B](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_UEFA_Nations_League_A#Group_4 "2020–21 UEFA Nations League A") in the [2nd Nations League](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_UEFA_Nations_League "2020–21 UEFA Nations League") due to their last-place finish (2 wins to 4 losses), failed to gain promotion to League A in the following Nations League, finishing 2nd in their group in the [3rd Nations League](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_UEFA_Nations_League_B "2022–23 UEFA Nations League B"), beaten by Scotland against whom they lost in [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow") in contrast to the play-off semi-final (0–3) and unable to beat the *Tartan Army* on neutral ground in the return leg (0–0).

In the [qualifiers for the Euro 2024](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024_qualifying_Group_C "UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying Group C"), Ukraine finished third in their highly competitive group, with four wins, two draws and two defeats. However, Italy finished ahead of the *Zbirna* only thanks to a favourable head-to-head record despite having the same number of points, while in the decisive Ukraine/Italy match (0–0) on neutral ground in [Leverkusen](/wiki/Leverkusen "Leverkusen"), Germany, a refereeing error was made to the Ukrainians' disadvantage with a penalty not whistled for an Italian foul at the end of match.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

Eligible for the play-offs as the best non-group winner, Ukraine initially defeated [Bosnia](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina_national_football_team "Bosnia and Herzegovina national football team") 2–1 away in the [semi-finals of the Path B play-offs](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024_qualifying_play-offs#Semi-finals_2 "UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying play-offs") on March 21, 2024.[[36]](#cite_note-37) Five days later, on March 26, 2024, Ukraine qualified for [Euro 2024](/wiki/Euro_2024 "Euro 2024") in Germany, beating Iceland 2–1 in the [play-off final](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024_qualifying_play-offs#Final_2 "UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying play-offs") at the [Wrocław Stadium](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw_Stadium "Wrocław Stadium") in [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland").[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39) In this decisive final, as in the semi-final against Bosnia, Ukraine conceded the opening goal on each occasion and managed to turn the match around, winning by a narrow margin at the very end. Having been drawn into Group E with Romania, Belgium and Slovakia, Ukraine finished level with all the other teams in the group on four points, but were knocked out on goal difference.

## Stadiums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ukraine_national_football_team&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Stadiums")]

Further information: [List of football stadiums in Ukraine](/wiki/List_of_football_stadiums_in_Ukraine "List of football stadiums in Ukraine")

Most matches are held at Kyiv's [Olimpiyskyi National Sports Complex](/wiki/Olimpiyskyi_National_Sports_Complex "Olimpiyskyi National Sports Complex").

During the Soviet era (before 1991), only three stadiums in Ukraine were used for official games:

* [Olimpiysky NSC](/wiki/Olimpiyskiy_National_Sports_Complex "Olimpiyskiy National Sports Complex") in Kyiv (known then as Republican Stadium)
* [BSS](/wiki/Black_Sea_Shipping_Company "Black Sea Shipping Company") Central Stadium in Odesa, the predecessor of [Chornomorets](/wiki/Chornomorets_Stadium "Chornomorets Stadium")
* [Lokomotiv Stadium](/wiki/Lokomotiv_Republican_Sports_Complex "Lokomotiv Republican Sports Complex") in [Simferopol](/wiki/Simferopol "Simferopol")

Since May 2022, due to the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine"), home game matches have been taking place in [Łódź](/wiki/%C5%81%C3%B3d%C5%BA "Łódź").[[39]](#cite_note-40)
